title: Safe Handling of Hazardous (Chemotherapy) Medications
subtitle: An RN is preparing to administer IV chemotherapy and must protect themselves and others from exposure — correct PPE, closed-system transfer, spill response, body-fluid precautions, and hazardous-waste disposal.

patient.name: Chemo Administration
patient.admitDate: Oncology unit. RN preparing to administer a hazardous antineoplastic (chemotherapy) drug. Focus is safe handling to prevent occupational exposure, including a spill scenario.

labs.r: Don't prime with drug at the bedside.

labs.v: Don PPE; contain and clean per policy.

labs.r: Not regular trash; sharps in sharps box.

labs.v: Wash skin soap+water; flush eyes 15 min.

nursesNote: An oncology RN is preparing to administer an IV hazardous (chemotherapy) drug. Hazardous medications can harm staff through skin contact, inhalation, or mucous-membrane exposure, so safe handling is required. The nurse wears the correct PPE (two pairs of chemotherapy-tested gloves and an impervious gown, plus face/eye protection if splashing is possible and respiratory protection if aerosolization is a risk), uses pharmacy-prepared doses with a closed-system transfer device, and keeps tubing primed by pharmacy with non-drug fluid. Doses are transported in sealed, labeled containers (never the pneumatic tube). A chemotherapy spill kit is kept accessible: for a spill, the nurse dons kit PPE, contains and cleans the area per policy, and disposes of all material in designated hazardous-waste containers. Because the drug is excreted in body fluids for about 48 hours, PPE is worn when handling urine, stool, emesis, and linens. After any skin contact the nurse washes immediately with soap and water (eyes flushed 15 minutes at the eyewash), reports the exposure, and seeks evaluation. Staff who are pregnant or lactating follow facility reproductive-risk policy.
providerOrders: Identify the medication as hazardous and gather correct PPE/supplies before handling
Wear double chemotherapy gloves and an impervious gown (face/respiratory protection if indicated)
Use pharmacy-prepared doses with a closed-system transfer device; transport sealed and labeled
Keep a chemotherapy spill kit accessible; manage spills and waste as hazardous
Use PPE for the patient's body fluids for ~48 hours; wash/flush and report any exposure

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What does safe hazardous-drug handling require?

opts.text: Hazardous drugs can expose staff through skin contact, inhalation, or mucous membranes
Two pairs of chemotherapy-tested gloves and an impervious gown are worn to handle them
A closed-system transfer device reduces drug aerosol and leakage
A chemotherapy spill kit should be readily accessible
Regular exam gloves alone are adequate for handling chemotherapy
The drug is excreted in body fluids for about 48 hours, so PPE is used for excreta
Hazardous drugs are prepared in a biologic safety cabinet, usually in pharmacy
Doses are transported in sealed, labeled containers, never by pneumatic tube
Hazardous waste and sharps go in designated containers, not the regular trash
After skin contact, wash immediately with soap and water and report the exposure
It is fine to prime the IV tubing with the drug at the bedside
opts.rat: False — pharmacy primes with non-drug fluid.

opts.text: Staff who are pregnant or lactating should follow facility reproductive-risk policy
Verify the drug is hazardous and gather PPE and supplies
Don double chemo gloves, gown, and face protection if splash risk
Use a closed-system transfer device with pharmacy-primed tubing
Administer per protocol with the spill kit accessible
Dispose of waste in hazardous containers and perform hand hygiene

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the action protect against exposure?

opts.text: Wear double chemotherapy gloves and an impervious gown
Use a closed-system transfer device
Wear gloves and gown when emptying the patient's urine within 48 hours
Dispose of hazardous waste in designated containers
Immediately wash skin with soap and water after contact
Send the chemotherapy dose through the pneumatic tube system
Prime the IV tubing with the drug at the bedside
Throw chemotherapy waste in the regular trash
Eat a snack while preparing the medication

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A drug spills on the floor — sequence the response
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Put on PPE from the chemotherapy spill kit
Contain the spill, absorbing from the outer edge inward
Clean the surface per facility policy
Dispose of all materials in hazardous-waste containers
Report and document the spill; restock the kit
The nurse dons double gloves and an impervious gown before handling the drug
A spill is managed with the spill kit and hazardous-waste container
PPE is worn when handling the patient's body fluids for 48 hours
After a splash, the nurse flushes the eye 15 minutes and reports the exposure
The nurse reuses one pair of gloves to save time
Chemotherapy tubing is thrown in the regular trash
A spill is wiped up without any PPE
A pregnant nurse handles the drug without checking reproductive-risk policy
